summary: I currently live in Puyallup, Washington, which has been one of my main residences alongside Seattle. I also had a residence in Tacoma within the past year, so I’m quite familiar with the Puget Sound area. Living in Puyallup has allowed me to stay connected to the communities and labor movements throughout the region.

I have a strong connection to Seattle, Washington, where I work at Harborview Medical Center in the Emergency Room. I’ve been part of the labor movement here for over twenty years, and Seattle is where I participate in my union activities and community groups. The city has been central to my professional and activist life.

I was born in San Diego, California, which is where my life began before moving to Washington state. Though I grew up elsewhere, San Diego remains an important part of my personal history. It’s the place of my birth and the starting point of my journey.

I regularly participate in what some would call the Pentecostal faith or denomination, though I consider myself non-denominational. My religious background is diverse, including Pentecostalism among other traditions, and I have been able to make my own choices about my faith. Pentecostalism is part of the spiritual heritage I engage with.

Catholicism is part of my family’s religious background, which I inherited alongside Protestant and Church of God traditions. While I chose my own path spiritually, the Catholic Church is an important part of my heritage. My mother allowed me to incorporate these diverse religious influences into my own faith journey.

Protestantism is one of the religious traditions present in my family history, along with Catholicism and Church of God. Though I identify as non-denominational, the Protestant background has shaped part of my spiritual roots. I appreciate the diversity of faith traditions in my heritage.

The Church of God is another faith tradition that is part of my family’s religious background. While I am non-denominational, this tradition, along with Catholicism and Protestantism, has influenced my spiritual heritage. I was fortunate to be able to choose my own faith path that incorporates these elements.

I have been working at Harborview Medical Center’s Emergency Room for nearly twenty-two years as a Patient Service Specialist. This is my primary place of employment, and I am deeply involved in the union there, Local 3488. Harborview has been central to my career and labor activism in Seattle.

Local 3488 is my union at Harborview Medical Center, and I currently serve as Vice President-Acting President. I have held many positions within this local union, from secretary to executive board member. Local 3488 has been a key part of my labor activism and leadership for many years.

entity: Washington Federation of State County Municipal Employees
summary: I am actively involved with the Washington Federation of State County Municipal Employees, serving on their statewide Executive Committee. This federation includes my local union, Local 3488, and represents a broader coalition of public employees in Washington. My leadership roles here reflect my long-term commitment to labor rights.

entity: American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees
summary: I represent the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME) as part of my union work. AFSCME is a national organization that supports public sector workers like myself. Being connected to AFSCME has allowed me to engage in labor activism beyond just the local and state levels.

I represent the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists (CBTU) both regionally and nationally, including serving on their women's committee. This organization connects me to a network of Black labor activists across the country. CBTU has been an important platform for my advocacy and community involvement within the labor movement.

Within the past year, I also had a residence in Tacoma, Washington, which is part of the greater Puget Sound area. Living in Tacoma expanded my connection to the region and its communities. It’s another place where I have been involved socially and politically alongside my work in Seattle and Puyallup.

I was initially employed at the University of Washington where I was a member of the Service Employees International Union (SEIU). During my time there, I was introduced to labor activism and met influential union leaders. The University was an important starting point for my union involvement before moving to Harborview Medical Center.

I was a member of the Service Employees International Union (SEIU) for several years, both at the University of Washington and previously at Swedish Medical Center. SEIU was my first union experience and helped me develop my passion for labor rights. I stayed with SEIU for about four to five years before transitioning to my current union.

Before working at Harborview, I was employed at Swedish Medical Center where I was a member of SEIU. This experience introduced me to union membership and the labor movement. Swedish Medical Center was an important early workplace in my career in healthcare and labor activism.

entity: American Federation of Labor and Congress of Industrial Organizations
summary: The American Federation of Labor and Congress of Industrial Organizations (AFL-CIO) is a major umbrella organization for labor unions, including those I am part of. I attended my first AFL-CIO meeting in Cleveland, where I was inspired by powerful women leaders. The AFL-CIO has a rich history tied to labor rights and civil rights movements, including Martin Luther King Jr.’s marches in the 1960s.

Martin Luther King Jr. was a pivotal figure in the labor movement, especially during the 1960s when he marched for workers' rights and fair wages. His leadership connected civil rights with labor rights, which deeply influences my activism today. I often reflect on his legacy as I participate in unions and community organizing.

April Sims is the first African American to hold a head position in the Washington state AFL-CIO, which is a significant milestone. I proudly recognize her leadership as a sign of progress within the labor movement here. Her role inspires me and others in the community to continue pushing for representation and equity.
cues: first African American head in Washington AFL-CIO

summary: At Harborview Medical Center, I often work closely with American Medical Response (AMR) personnel as they bring patients into the Emergency Room. I am usually at the first point of contact greeting medics and coordinating patient transfers. AMR is a critical partner in the emergency care process where I work daily.
